content.text: Why a Components Catalog Matters Before Architecture

A solution blueprint is only as good as its component vocabulary. Without a shared catalog that defines what each component does, its interfaces, its dependencies, and its failure modes, architecture diagrams become ambiguous: different engineers interpret 'memory layer' differently, 'tool registry' means different things to frontend and backend teams, and the system that gets built does not match what was designed. A component catalog is the shared vocabulary that makes blueprints unambiguous and auditable.

This topic defines the ten canonical component categories in an enterprise agentic AI system, provides a specification format for each component, and shows how a component registry enforces dependency correctness at deployment time. The goal is not to prescribe a specific implementation for each component but to define each component's responsibilities and interface contracts so that teams can select and assemble components reliably.

attrs.alt: Blueprint components catalog showing ten categories: Orchestration, Tool Registry, Memory/Context, Safety/Guardrails, Observability, Model Gateway, Prompt Management, Data Connectors, Auth/Policy, Deployment Runtime
attrs.title: Blueprint components catalog showing ten categories: Orchestration, Tool Registry, Memory/Context, Safety/Guardrails, Observability, Model Gateway, Prompt Management, Data Connectors, Auth/Policy, Deployment Runtime

content.text: The ten categories are not optional features — every enterprise deployment needs all ten. The variance is in how each category is implemented: a startup might use a lightweight in-process implementation while an enterprise deploys dedicated microservices. The category boundaries remain stable; the implementation choices vary.
Decompose tasks, route to agents, manage conversation state
Catalog available tools with schemas, enforce permissions, apply rate limits
Provide working memory for active sessions, persistent store for long-term recall
Read/write API with namespace and TTL
Classify inputs, validate outputs, scrub PII, route to HitL
Emit traces, record token costs, run eval pipeline, route alerts
Abstract provider APIs, retry/fallback, cache, attribute costs
Version-control prompt templates, inject variables, support A/B routing
Connect agents to databases, APIs, files, and event streams
Authenticate users and agents, enforce RBAC, audit all access
AuthZ check API + audit event bus
Container lifecycle, configuration, secrets, health checks

A component without a formal spec creates two problems. First, it makes handoff between teams ambiguous: if the platform team builds the Model Gateway and an application team consumes it, both need an agreed contract. Second, it makes failure mode handling inconsistent: without a spec that lists failure modes and mitigations, each consuming team implements its own error handling — often incorrectly.

The specification format used here covers six areas: responsibilities (what the component does), interfaces (the APIs it exposes and consumes), dependencies (what it requires), SLA (the performance and availability commitments it makes), failure modes (the specific ways it can fail and how failures are detected and mitigated), and observability (the metrics, traces, and logs it emits). This format is compact enough to live in a single YAML file and detailed enough to support independent implementation.

# blueprint-component-spec.yaml
# Specification for the Model Gateway component

component:
  id: model-gateway
  version: "2.1"
  category: orchestration
  owner: platform-team

responsibilities:
  - "Abstract provider-specific APIs behind a uniform interface"
  - "Apply retry with exponential backoff on transient errors"
  - "Route to fallback model when primary exceeds error budget"
  - "Attribute token costs to caller_id and session_id"
  - "Cache deterministic responses by input hash (temperature=0 only)"

interfaces:
  inbound:
    - name: complete
      method: POST
      path: /v1/complete
      request_schema:
        model: string          # logical model name: "gpt-4o" | "claude-3-7-sonnet"
        messages: array
        temperature: number    # 0.0–2.0
        max_tokens: integer
        caller_id: string      # required for cost attribution
        session_id: string     # required for trace correlation
        idempotency_key: string  # optional; enables caching
      response_schema:
        content: string
        usage:
          input_tokens: integer
          output_tokens: integer
          cost_usd: number
        provider: string       # actual provider used (may differ on fallback)
        cached: boolean

  outbound:
    - name: openai_api
      base_url: "https://api.openai.com/v1"
      auth: bearer_token_from_secret_store
    - name: anthropic_api
      base_url: "https://api.anthropic.com/v1"
      auth: x-api-key_from_secret_store

dependencies:
  required:
    - secret-store        # for API keys
    - distributed-tracer  # for span injection
    - cost-meter          # for usage recording
  optional:
    - response-cache      # for idempotent calls

sla:
  p50_latency_ms: 800
  p99_latency_ms: 4000
  availability: 0.999
  error_budget_per_month_minutes: 43  # 99.9% = 43.8 min/month downtime

failure_modes:
  - id: FM-001
    scenario: "Primary provider rate limit"
    detection: "HTTP 429 or error code rate_limit_exceeded"
    mitigation: "Immediate retry on fallback model; alert if fallback also rate-limited"
  - id: FM-002
    scenario: "Primary provider outage"
    detection: "HTTP 503 or connection timeout > 10s"
    mitigation: "Circuit breaker opens; all traffic routes to fallback"
  - id: FM-003
    scenario: "Response truncated by max_tokens"
    detection: "finish_reason == 'length'"
    mitigation: "Return partial response with finish_reason in metadata; caller decides retry"

observability:
  metrics:
    - "model_gateway.request.count{provider, model, caller_id, status}"
    - "model_gateway.latency.p50{provider, model}"
    - "model_gateway.latency.p99{provider, model}"
    - "model_gateway.cost.usd{caller_id, model}"
    - "model_gateway.cache.hit_rate{model}"
  traces: "OTEL span per request with provider and model attributes"
  logs: "Structured JSON; include caller_id and session_id on every line"

The component registry has two jobs beyond simple bookkeeping. First, it validates the dependency graph at deployment time: if a required component is not registered, the deployment fails with a clear message rather than silently at runtime. Second, it produces a startup order via topological sort: components that have no dependencies start first, followed by those that depend only on already-started components. This replaces ad-hoc startup scripts with a principled order derived from the component specifications.

The Model Gateway is the component that most directly affects system latency and cost. Its circuit breaker prevents cascading failures when a provider has an outage, its caching layer can dramatically reduce costs for frequently-repeated deterministic calls, and its provider routing table determines which model runs when the primary is unavailable.

import asyncio
import hashlib
import json
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any


class ProviderStatus(Enum):
    HEALTHY = "healthy"
    DEGRADED = "degraded"
    CIRCUIT_OPEN = "circuit_open"


@dataclass
class CompletionRequest:
    model: str           # logical model name
    messages: list[dict]
    temperature: float = 0.0
    max_tokens: int = 4096
    caller_id: str = ""
    session_id: str = ""
    idempotency_key: str = ""


@dataclass
class CompletionResponse:
    content: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost_usd: float
    provider: str        # actual provider used
    model: str           # actual model used
    cached: bool = False
    finish_reason: str = "stop"
    latency_ms: float = 0.0


class ModelGateway:
    # Unified interface to multiple LLM providers with retry, fallback, caching.

    PROVIDER_ROUTES = {
        "gpt-4o": [("openai", "gpt-4o"), ("anthropic", "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022")],
        "claude-3-7-sonnet": [("anthropic", "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219"), ("openai", "gpt-4o")],
        "claude-3-5-haiku": [("anthropic", "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022")],
    }

    COST_PER_1M_TOKENS = {
        ("openai", "gpt-4o"): (2.50, 10.00),         # input, output per 1M
        ("anthropic", "claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219"): (3.00, 15.00),
        ("anthropic", "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"): (3.00, 15.00),
        ("anthropic", "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022"): (0.80, 4.00),
    }

    def __init__(self, provider_clients: dict[str, Any], cache: dict | None = None):
        self._clients = provider_clients
        self._cache: dict[str, CompletionResponse] = cache if cache is not None else {}
        self._circuit_states: dict[str, ProviderStatus] = {}
        self._circuit_error_counts: dict[str, int] = {}
        self.CIRCUIT_OPEN_THRESHOLD = 5  # consecutive errors before opening

    def _cache_key(self, req: CompletionRequest) -> str | None:
        if req.temperature != 0.0:
            return None  # Only cache deterministic calls
        if req.idempotency_key:
            return req.idempotency_key
        key_data = json.dumps({
            "model": req.model,
            "messages": req.messages,
            "max_tokens": req.max_tokens,
        }, sort_keys=True)
        return hashlib.sha256(key_data.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]

    def _cost(self, provider: str, model: str, input_tok: int, output_tok: int) -> float:
        rates = self.COST_PER_1M_TOKENS.get((provider, model), (0, 0))
        return (input_tok * rates[0] + output_tok * rates[1]) / 1_000_000

    def _is_circuit_open(self, provider: str) -> bool:
        return self._circuit_states.get(provider) == ProviderStatus.CIRCUIT_OPEN

    def _record_error(self, provider: str) -> None:
        self._circuit_error_counts[provider] = self._circuit_error_counts.get(provider, 0) + 1
        if self._circuit_error_counts[provider] >= self.CIRCUIT_OPEN_THRESHOLD:
            self._circuit_states[provider] = ProviderStatus.CIRCUIT_OPEN

    def _record_success(self, provider: str) -> None:
        self._circuit_error_counts[provider] = 0
        self._circuit_states[provider] = ProviderStatus.HEALTHY

    async def complete(self, req: CompletionRequest) -> CompletionResponse:
        cache_key = self._cache_key(req)
        if cache_key and cache_key in self._cache:
            resp = self._cache[cache_key]
            return CompletionResponse(**{**resp.__dict__, "cached": True})

        routes = self.PROVIDER_ROUTES.get(req.model)
        if not routes:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {req.model}")

        last_exc: Exception | None = None
        for provider, native_model in routes:
            if self._is_circuit_open(provider):
                continue
            client = self._clients.get(provider)
            if client is None:
                continue
            try:
                start = time.monotonic()
                raw = await client.complete(
                    model=native_model,
                    messages=req.messages,
                    temperature=req.temperature,
                    max_tokens=req.max_tokens,
                )
                latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
                self._record_success(provider)
                cost = self._cost(provider, native_model, raw["input_tokens"], raw["output_tokens"])
                resp = CompletionResponse(
                    content=raw["content"],
                    input_tokens=raw["input_tokens"],
                    output_tokens=raw["output_tokens"],
                    cost_usd=cost,
                    provider=provider,
                    model=native_model,
                    finish_reason=raw.get("finish_reason", "stop"),
                    latency_ms=latency_ms,
                )
                if cache_key:
                    self._cache[cache_key] = resp
                return resp
            except Exception as exc:
                self._record_error(provider)
                last_exc = exc
                continue

        raise RuntimeError(f"All providers failed for {req.model}. Last: {last_exc}")

A component's interface must be more stable than its implementation. Implementation details — the specific retry library used, the cache backend, the database schema — can change without coordination with consuming teams. Interface changes require coordination. This means that interface design is the most important architectural decision for each component: choosing the right interface abstraction is harder to change than choosing the right implementation.
All component interfaces use versioned paths: /v1/complete, /v2/complete — never break /v1 when adding /v2
Interfaces accept and return structured types with explicit schemas (Pydantic models, JSON Schema, or OpenAPI specs)
Error responses follow a standard format: {error_code, error_message, retry_after} — no free-form error strings
All requests include caller_id and session_id for cost attribution and trace correlation
Interface changes require a deprecation period: document the breaking change 30 days before removing the old version

Component interfaces must specify whether they are synchronous (caller blocks until result) or asynchronous (caller gets a job ID and polls or receives a callback). The choice is driven by latency requirements and decoupling needs. Safety guardrail checks must be synchronous because the orchestrator cannot proceed until it knows whether the input is safe. Model completion calls should offer both: synchronous for interactive use cases where latency is user-visible, and asynchronous (streaming or polling) for batch processing.
Safety check interfaces: synchronous, < 50ms P99 — if this is slow, it blocks every agent turn
Model completion: offer synchronous (blocking) and streaming (SSE/WebSocket) interfaces
Eval pipeline: asynchronous; results are consumed via a callback or polling endpoint
Audit logging: fire-and-forget async; never block the calling component on audit log writes
Cost attribution: asynchronous batch writes; real-time attribution is an approximation from the gateway

Not every component category requires a separate service. In smaller deployments, several component categories can be implemented as modules within a single service. The decision to extract a component into a separate service should be driven by team ownership, scale requirements, and independent deployment needs — not by a desire to follow a microservices pattern for its own sake.
Single service; extract only if team boundary requires it
Gateway as shared platform service; orchestration per use case
Multiple orchestration services per use case
Dedicated gateway cluster with regional replicas
Full platform team ownership of gateway and observability; use-case teams own orchestrators
Start with the minimum number of services that satisfy team ownership requirements
Extract a component when its scaling needs differ from the components it currently shares a process with
Extract a component when two different teams need to deploy it on different cadences
Never extract a component purely for technical elegance — every service boundary adds operational overhead
Document the component boundary rationale in the ADR using the ownership and scaling criteria above

A component catalog defines responsibilities, interfaces, dependencies, SLAs, and failure modes for each of the ten component categories — without this vocabulary, architecture diagrams are ambiguous.
The component registry validates dependency graphs at deployment time: register all required dependencies before starting the system, and use topological sort for startup ordering.

Interface stability is more important than implementation stability: version all interfaces, follow a deprecation process for breaking changes, and always include caller_id and session_id for attribution.
The Model Gateway's circuit breaker, provider fallback routing, and response cache are the primary mechanisms for cost control and reliability in multi-provider deployments.
Extract components into separate services when team ownership or independent scaling requires it — not by default.
